analysis:
- Strongest designs (Heuristics 2–6) converge on the same structural pattern: prune dominated points, build a single shared Monte‑Carlo sample pool, precompute point→sample domination (boolean masks or sparse index lists), run a lazy‑greedy max‑heap using estimated marginal gains with incremental coverage updates, then do a bounded exact or mask‑based local polish on a compact shortlist. This combination reuses heavy work (samples, masks), avoids repeated full hypervolume calls, and gives predictable runtime vs. quality tradeoffs.
- Heuristic 1 attains the best quality by brute‑force exact‑HV operations (add/remove worst by exact contribution, then exhaustive swap LS). That yields strong final HV but at high cost because it repeatedly computes full hypervolumes for many subsets (avoidable repeated work, many HV object constructions).
- Middle performers differ in pragmatic choices that affect speed/accuracy: M (sample count) scaling rules, block sizes for memory safety, thresholding for when to build sparse index lists, shortlist sizing, and tie‑breaking weights (box‑volume vs. marginal). Those parameters explain small score differences: larger M and more aggressive polish give better HV but slower runs; aggressive sparsity and smaller shortlists speed up runtime with modest HV loss.
- Weaker heuristics (7, 10, 11, 12) either: (a) recompute exact hypervolume per candidate inside large loops (very costly), (b) omit nondominated pruning, (c) use small/naive sampling or diversity seeding that isn’t HV‑aware, or (d) perform many Python‑level loops and repeated np.where searches. These patterns cause either wasted CPU time or poorer HV estimates.
- Common avoidable overheads across submissions: repeated creation/calls to hypervolume objects inside inner loops; failing to reuse precomputed masks and covered arrays; converting between Python lists/sets and numpy arrays frequently; naive per‑point loops instead of block/vectorized operations; building full dense boolean mats when sparse lists would suffice.
- Best pragmatic tradeoffs observed: deterministic, modestly large stratified/jittered sampling; blockwise mask computation to limit memory; building sparse index lists when average domination is low; lazy greedy heap with re‑estimation only when needed; use box‑volume tie‑breakers; restrict exact HV to a small shortlist (and cache/have single hv object per candidate slice). Those yield near‑top HV with far less runtime than global exact HV search.
experience: Prune dominated candidates, share one deterministic MC sample pool, compute point→sample masks in memory‑safe blocks, and build sparse index lists when cheap. Use a lazy‑greedy heap with incremental coverage updates and box‑volume tie‑breaks, then run bounded exact HV only on a small, scored shortlist. Cache hypervolume objects, avoid per‑candidate full HV inside big loops, vectorize blocks, and adapt sample count to (k, D, N) for good speed/quality balance.
current_reflection: Prune dominated candidates, share one deterministic MC sample pool, compute point→sample masks in memory‑safe blocks, and build sparse index lists when cheap. Use a lazy‑greedy heap with incremental coverage updates and box‑volume tie‑breaks, then run bounded exact HV only on a small, scored shortlist. Cache hypervolume objects, avoid per‑candidate full HV inside big loops, vectorize blocks, and adapt sample count to (k, D, N) for good speed/quality balance.
bad_reflections: Prune dominated points, precompute shared MC samples and boolean masks in memory‑safe blocks, then run a lazy greedy heap with incremental covered masks. Use sparse index lists when appropriate, keep sample size proportional to k and D, and restrict exact HV calls to a tiny shortlisted pool for bounded swap polishing.

Prune dominated points, build one blockwise MC sample pool, and store per‑point coverage as boolean masks or sparse index lists. Use a lazy max‑heap (estimated gains → cheap true‑gain counts) to pick k, tie‑break with box‑volume, then run a bounded exact/approx swap polish on a small shortlist. Cap M, use block processing, and seed RNGs for reproducibility. Avoid repeated full HV calls inside inner loops.

Prune dominated points, reuse a single deterministic MC sample pool, compute masks in blocks, and run a lazy greedy heap with incremental true‑gain updates (sparse lists when sparse). Cache gains and avoid per‑candidate exact HV calls; restrict exact HV to a small shortlisted pool and cap iterations. These choices give reliable HV quality with predictable, much lower runtime.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: dominated-pruning, deterministic-MC, blockwise-masks, sparse-indices, lazy-greedy-heap, incremental-gains, box-volume-tiebreak, shortlist-HV, HV-caching, vectorization, adaptive-samples
- Advice: prune dominated candidates; build one seeded MC pool; compute point→sample masks in memory‑safe blocks and use sparse index lists when sparse; run a lazy greedy heap with incremental coverage updates and box‑volume tie‑breaks; shortlist and run bounded exact HV with cached objects; tune sample count by (k,D,N) and vectorize blocks.
- Avoid: per‑candidate full hypervolume calls inside big loops, unseeded MC pools, allocating full boolean matrices at once, recomputing masks/gains unnecessarily.
- Explanation: reduces memory and wasted work, yields reproducible estimates, keeps runtime predictable, and concentrates expensive exact HV only where it matters for quality.
